result:
The rain comes sideways in Chicago. Not the polite English rain that knows how to fall straight down, apologizing for the inconvenience. This rain has attitude. It slashes across Division Street like it has a destination and a schedule, and you are just an obstacle, a soggy pedestrian obstacle, standing there with your useless umbrella that you bought at a CVS on a hopeful Tuesday in April when the sky was merely threatening.

I watch it from the window of the laundromat. The machines hum their industrial hymn, and the fluorescent lights cast everything in that particular shade of institutional white that makes even clean clothes look suspicious. A woman two seats over is reading a romance novel with a cover that features a man with no shirt and an improbable number of abs. He is holding a sword. I respect her commitment to the genre. She does not look up. She does not look at the rain. She is somewhere in the Scottish Highlands, I assume, falling in love with a man whose chest defies physics.

My clothes are spinning. The spin cycle is a kind of meditation, if you think about it. Everything getting pushed around, thrown this way and that, agitated, but eventually arriving at some form of cleanliness. I envy the simplicity. My own spin cycle has been running for thirty-seven years and I still can't tell if I'm cleaner or just more thoroughly circulated.

The woman's phone rings. She answers it without looking up from her abs-and-sword man. "I told you I'd be here until eight," she says. Her voice has that particular Chicago flatness, the vowels compressed like accordion folds, the consonants sharp as ice. "The towels aren't done yet. No, I'm not leaving. You come here if you want to talk about it." She hangs up. She reads. The rain slashes.

There's a kid sitting on the floor by the dryers, drawing on a brown paper bag with a blue crayon. He's maybe six. His sneakers are untied and the tongue of one is sticking out like a dog begging for table scraps. He draws a house. A square with a triangle on top. A door. Two windows. This is how we all start, isn't it? With the simplest shape of shelter. Before we learn about mortgage rates and property taxes and homeowners associations and the particular cruelty of property insurance in a state that exists between a lake and a tornado corridor. Before we learn that the house we draw with a blue crayon at age six is not a house we will ever afford to live in. The kid doesn't know yet. He is just drawing. The house is just a house.

I think about my first apartment. Studio, $450 a month, Rogers Park, above a Thai restaurant. The kitchen was a strip of counter along one wall, and the bathroom had a showerhead that produced water with the enthusiasm of a tired bureaucrat dispensing stamps. But it was mine. I was twenty-two and I had a key that opened a door that closed behind me and locked out the world, and the world at twenty-two is a thing you very much want locked out.

Now I'm thirty-seven and I own nothing that locks. I rent this body, this mind, this particular arrangement of neuroses and habits and small joys that constitute a self. The lease is indefinite and the landlord is capricious, but I've lived here long enough to know the quirks of the property. I know that the left knee creaks when the weather drops below forty. I know that the mind tends toward rumination at 2 AM, spinning like the washers on their final cycle, agitating nothing into nothing. I know that the heart, despite its obvious structural flaws, continues to pump. It continues to pump.

The kid finishes his house. He holds it up to the fluorescent light and regards it with the serious aesthetic judgment of a six-year-old. Then he folds the bag carefully, creases it, puts it in his pocket. He has drawn a house he cannot live in, and he knows this, and he has saved the drawing anyway. I want to tell him something. I want to say: keep drawing houses. Keep folding them into your pocket. The world will try to unfold you, will try to flatten you into a single surface, will try to make you forget that you once contained multitudes. But the creases remain. The fold marks are permanent. You will always be more than the single plane the world demands you occupy.

I don't say this. I'm a stranger in a laundromat, and strangers who talk to children are categorized by society into one of two groups: friendly or suspicious. There is no third category for "person who simply wants to share a small philosophical observation about the nature of folded paper and the persistence of dreams." So I say nothing. I just watch him tie his shoe, the one with the tongue sticking out, and he loops the laces around each other in a way I've never seen before, some complicated knot that looks like it would take a diagram to untangle, and I think: he's already learning. He's already learning that the world requires you to invent your own methods of holding things together.

The woman with the romance novel closes her book. She looks at her phone. She looks at the dryers. She stands up and pulls her clothes from the machine, warm and staticky, and she folds each item with military precision — corners aligned, edges flush, the whole stack compressed into a rectangle that fits perfectly inside her laundry bag. She is a person who imposes order on unordered things. She is a person who makes the world slightly more manageable through the simple act of folding. I admire this. I have never been able to fold a towel correctly. My towels always end up rolled or bunched or shoved into the cabinet in a way that suggests I am not welcome in the kingdom of linen.

She leaves. She does not say goodbye. She does not need to. We have shared a space, a time, a fluorescent hum, and that is enough. In Chicago, you learn that intimacy is not always verbal. Sometimes it is just the act of sitting near another human body, both of you watching the same rain, both of you waiting for the same cycle to end.

My clothes are done. I pull them out, warm and damp, and I stuff them into my bag without folding them. Let them be unordered. Let them be exactly as messy as the mind that produced them. I walk out into the rain, and the rain does not care about my philosophy. It hits my face and my hands and my useless umbrella, and I think: this is what it means to be alive. To be struck by something you cannot control, to be made wet against your will, to keep walking anyway, holding your folded and your unfolded things, your creased and your uncreased dreams, yourabs-and-sword romances and your brown paper bag houses, all of it jumbled together in the bag you carry through the sideways rain, all of it yours, all of it rented, all of it spinning and spinning toward some final cycle that will end, eventually, in stillness.

The laundromat light stays on behind me. The kid is still there, drawing something new. The machines are still humming. And I am walking home through the rain that comes sideways, the rain that does not apologize, the rain that is exactly what it is without pretense or politeness, and for this, for the brute honesty of its slash, I love it. I love this city and its oblique weather. I love the way it refuses to fall straight down.

message.reasoning: The user wants me to write whatever comes to me - 1000 words of free writing. Let me just let my mind go and write something authentic, The deadline is pressing, but I'll write what flows naturally.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
